Q1: My study routine was honestly chaotic but intentional. I work part-time and have family responsibilities, so I couldn't follow a traditional schedule. I'd study in intense bursts—sometimes six or seven hours straight on my days off, then nothing for a few days. I'm a visual learner, so I'd create mind maps and diagrams from the lecture content, which took time but helped everything stick. I learned to maximize every spare moment I had, even if it looked irregular from the outside.
Q2: My pattern varied significantly based on what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I could dedicate full days to studying, other weeks I barely managed a few hours. Deadlines definitely pushed me to prioritize certain courses, but I tried to stay ahead on exam preparation since that's where I perform best. The unpredictability was stressful, but I've learned to adapt and make it work somehow.
Q3: I usually logged in when I needed to check for new announcements or download materials for offline study. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to participate in discussions because connecting with peers helped me feel less isolated. Honestly, with limited internet access at times, I had to be strategic about when I went online—I'd make a list of what I needed and try to get everything in one session.
Q4: Not really a daily routine, but I developed a pattern of logging in at the beginning of each week to download everything I'd need. It became like a weekly ritual—Sunday evenings, I'd grab all the videos and readings, then work through them offline. The peer support group I joined helped make this feel more natural because we'd coordinate our schedules together.
Q5: The video lectures were invaluable because I could download them and watch repeatedly while taking visual notes. Discussion forums were also helpful—reading how other students understood concepts gave me different perspectives. The peer interactions, even though they happened partly outside the LMS, originated from connections I made there. Having visual diagrams and flowcharts in the course materials really matched how my brain processes information.
Q6: I often skipped the optional readings and some of the supplementary materials because I simply didn't have time. With 18 courses and limited resources, I had to be ruthless about prioritizing. I focused on what would directly help my exam performance since that's where I could really demonstrate my understanding. Some interactive activities required stable internet I didn't always have, so those got neglected too.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to exam content or helped me build a deeper understanding I could apply. I also valued activities that let me interact with peers because feeling connected reduced my anxiety about the course. If something felt like busy work or repetitive, I'd skip it—I couldn't afford to waste energy when resources were already stretched thin.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I had to check off, which sometimes made them feel burdensome when I was already overwhelmed. Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't always afford time-wise, even when they seemed interesting. But occasionally, when I did engage with optional content, it felt more enjoyable because there was no pressure attached. The freedom made a difference in how I approached them mentally.
Q9: Absolutely—most of my studying happened offline. I'd download lecture videos, PDFs, and any materials I could access, then study from my printed notes and hand-drawn diagrams. I used textbooks from the library and shared notes with peers through messaging apps. My actual learning happened away from the computer, surrounded by color-coded notes and visual summaries I'd created.
Q10: My exam preparation was almost entirely invisible in the LMS. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, practicing problems from textbooks, and quizzing myself using flashcards I made. The peer study sessions we organized happened over phone calls or in person when possible. All that intense preparation that led to my high exam scores wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online to make sure it downloaded correctly, then watch it three or four more times offline while pausing to take detailed notes. Same with readings—I'd access them once, print them if I could, and then annotate them heavily over several days. My single access often represented the beginning of hours of offline engagement.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods when everything felt overwhelming. When my stress peaked and fear about failing crept in, I'd sometimes freeze and avoid logging in altogether. Financial pressures and family issues would compound the academic stress, making it hard to focus. During those times, my peer support connections were lifelines—they'd check in on me and help me get back on track.
Q13: Constantly. When I was exhausted from work or dealing with family responsibilities, the LMS felt like another demand I couldn't meet. Confusion about certain topics made me avoid those modules temporarily because I didn't have energy to struggle through them alone. Competing responsibilities meant I had to triage ruthlessly—sometimes the LMS lost out to more immediate needs, even though I knew I was falling behind on assignments.
Q14: Yes, sometimes I'd scroll through materials feeling completely disconnected, my mind elsewhere worrying about bills or family. I'd have the video playing but realize I'd absorbed nothing and have to start over later. Those moments were frustrating because I was technically 'present' but not really learning. My anxiety made it hard to concentrate even when I carved out time to study.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is why my completion rate is lower than I'd like. The fear of not doing them perfectly sometimes paralyzed me—I'd rather delay than submit something substandard. I knew exams were my strength, so I unconsciously prioritized exam prep over assignments. Looking back, I wish I'd been less scared of imperfect work.
Q16: Deadlines created intense pressure that both motivated and terrified me. I'd often have bursts of LMS activity right before due dates, downloading everything I needed for a final push. But sometimes deadlines overlapped with work shifts or family emergencies, and I'd have to make painful choices about what to sacrifice. The deadline-driven approach wasn't ideal but it's how I survived.
Q17: Feedback was really important to me when I received it, though it sometimes came too late to help with the next assignment. Positive feedback boosted my confidence and made me more likely to engage with similar activities. Critical feedback was harder to process given my anxiety, but I tried to use it constructively. My peer support group helped me interpret feedback when I was too stressed to see it clearly.
Q18: The course structure with its flexibility allowed me to manage my complicated life, which I appreciated. However, having 18 courses meant I couldn't give each one the attention it deserved. The modular design helped me prioritize what to focus on each week. I wish there had been more guidance on what was truly essential versus nice-to-have.
Q19: The availability of downloadable content made engagement possible for someone with my internet limitations. However, some interactive elements required being online simultaneously with others, which was difficult to coordinate. The discussion forums were accessible, which helped. What made it harder was when deadlines clustered together across multiple courses—that's when I felt most scared and overwhelmed.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for understanding, trying different approaches, or feeling lost about what's actually required. High activity could mean they're struggling and seeking help repeatedly, or they're anxious and checking things obsessively. It could also be that they're engaged intellectually but facing barriers to completing the actual submissions—life circumstances, perfectionism, or confusion about requirements.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score was over 94, but my LMS activity was minimal because I studied extensively offline. The system can't see the hours I spent with printed materials, the peer discussions over phone, or the visual diagrams covering my walls. Performance and visible activity aren't the same thing—especially for students who've learned to be resourceful with limited resources.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of access, timing, and frequency—useful for identifying students who might be struggling or disengaged. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, peer learning that happens elsewhere, and the emotional state behind the numbers. They can't capture whether someone is genuinely learning or just clicking through. They definitely can't show resilience or determination.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until it makes sense, not just completing tasks. It's about caring enough to persist when things get difficult and finding ways to connect concepts to real understanding. For me, engagement also includes the peer connections—learning isn't solitary. True engagement is internal and emotional as much as it is behavioral.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt, or how much courage it took to keep going despite that fear. It wouldn't capture the late-night study sessions with printed notes, the peer support calls that kept me motivated, or the visual learning strategies I developed independently. It can't show that behind my low assignment completion was someone fighting hard with limited resources, not someone who didn't care.
